romania.title: Cinci arhitecți români, ambasadori ai educației de mediu construit în Polonia
romania.summary: În perioada 24-26 aprilie, orașul polonez Kazimierz Dolny a găzduit cea de-a treia ediție a Forumului Internațional al Educației de Mediu Construit. Prezența arhitecților români a fost posibilă cu sprijinul… Articolul Cinci arhitecți români, ambasadori ai educației de mediu construit în Polonia apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: One World Romania 19 continuă online
romania.summary: După 10 zile pline de proiecții, discuții publice și evenimente speciale, One World Romania 19 se mută online. Până pe 31 mai, poți viziona de acasă 15 dintre filmele documentare care au fost… Articolul One World Romania 19 continuă online apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Asociația Învață pentru Tine este o organizație dedicată sprijinirii copiilor și tinerilor proveniți din medii vulnerabile, oferindu-le acces gratuit la educație, dezvoltare personală și oportunități reale pentru viitor luptand in acest mod si cu abandonul scolar.
romania.summary: In acest moment peste 120 copii din întreaga țară, care provin din familii cu probleme financiare, beneficiază de meditații online, gratuite, prin proiectul „Învaț pentru mine, pentru viitorul meu”. Articolul Asociația Învață pentru Tine este o organizație dedicată sprijinirii copiilor și tinerilor proveniți din medii vulnerabile, oferindu-le acces gratuit la educație, dezvoltare personală și oportunități reale pentru viitor luptand in acest mod si cu abandonul scolar. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public” și-a desemnat câștigătorii
romania.summary: Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public”, organizat de The European Digital Innovation Hub in Transilvania (TEDIHT), s-a încheiat astăzi, odată cu desfășurarea Demo Day, marcând finalul unui parcurs de aproape… Articolul Hackathonul „Soluții pentru digitalizarea sectorului public” și-a desemnat câștigătorii apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Două producții noi care aduc în spațiul imersiv mitologia greacă și lumea subacvatică, într-un format creat pentru copii și părinți deopotrivă. Din 9 mai 2026, MINA, Museum of Immersive New… Articolul MINA lansează din 9 mai 2026 două noi experiențe imersive: „Zeii Olimpului” și „În Inima Oceanului” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Pentru că plouă afară, suveraniștii vor mânca de 1 Mai mici aduși de livratori nepalezi
romania.summary: Chiar dacă plouă afară, asta nu înseamnă că tradiția micilor de 1 Mai trebuie să fie abandonată. ȘI mai ales suveraniștii români insistă că e nevoie de o mobilizare suplimentară pentru păstrarea acestei tradiții autentic românești. „Nu ești român adevărat dacă nu mănânci mici de 1 Mai”, a proclamat Giani-Ilarie Lemandroi, un suveranist din Berceni. […] The post Pentru că plouă afară, suveraniștii vor mânca de 1 Mai mici aduși de livratori nepalezi appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
romania.summary: Lansat în 2006 ca o satiră SF a unei lumi posibile, dar improbabile, filmul Idiocracy a ajuns să fie perceput azi cu totul altfel. Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări
romania.summary: Om cu principii solide, Nicușor Dan rămâne un mediator indiferent de situația în care se regăsește. Chiar și azi, când Sorin Grindeanu l-a trimis după țigări, președintele și-a demonstrat încă o dată abilitățile de mediator și, în locul unui refuz categoric, a ales să-i medieze liderului PSD pachetul de țigări cerut. Nicușor Dan și-a mediat […] The post Președinte-mediator! Grindeanu l-a trimis pe Nicușor să-i medieze un pachet de țigări appeared first on Times New Roman .

Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

romania.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
romania.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

romania.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
romania.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Părinte Social
romania.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: In cesarean birth, sex-specific effects occur in microbiota
medical.summary: The effects of cesarean delivery on the newborn microbiota appear to vary according to sex. A research team coordinated by INRAE has demonstrated this in a mouse model, showing increased susceptibility to colonic inflammation and a compromised gut barrier in males. These findings are published in Gut Microbes.

medical.title: A study in 1.4 million women expands knowledge on endometriosis and its biological complexity
medical.summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease that affects approximately one in ten women of reproductive age—around 190 million worldwide—remains poorly understood from a biological perspective, which has historically hindered both its accurate diagnosis and the development of effective treatments. Now, an international study published in Nature Genetics provides new data to better understand the genetic basis and mechanisms involved in this condition.

medical.title: MRI reveals cerebrospinal fluid shifts after mild brain injury
medical.summary: Researchers at University of Tsukuba have found that cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) microdynamic motion shows region-specific alterations after mild traumatic brain injury (TBI). Using a specialized magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) technique, the team noninvasively visualized these CSF changes, which have been difficult to quantify with conventional imaging. The approach is expected to advance the understanding of the relationship between post-traumatic brain conditions and cognitive function. The study is published in Frontiers in Neuroscience.

medical.title: Treatment of rare childhood epilepsy could begin before birth
medical.summary: Research has shown early diagnosis and treatment of epilepsy disorders can improve outcomes. The question of when to administer treatment so it gets ahead of the disease, however, has remained stubbornly elusive. A new Northwestern University study suggests intervention could start during pregnancy—as early as 15 weeks gestation—well before symptoms appear, highlighting the potential benefit of treating certain epilepsy disorders as early as possible.

medical.title: No live animal testing needed: Lab-grown airway organoids reveal viral infection in wildlife species
medical.summary: Experimental infections, where a pathogen is introduced into the host body to see its effects in action, are considered the gold standard for assessing how vulnerable a host is, offering clear insights into how a pathogen causes disease and spreads through populations. This approach requires deliberately infecting live animals, which not only raises clear ethical concerns but also often proves impractical for many wildlife species, especially endangered ones, where conservation efforts take precedence. Scientists have found a potential solution to this problem.

medical.title: Doomscrolling: Tips to stop the scroll, protect your mental health
medical.summary: "I should stop." Have you ever said those words to yourself as you scrolled through one disheartening online post after another? This phenomenon is popularly known as doomscrolling. You may wonder: Why is it so difficult to stop doomscrolling once you start? More importantly, what can you do to break free from the negative spiral? Craig Sawchuk, Ph.D., a psychologist at Mayo Clinic in Rochester, offers insights.

medical.title: Braille is everywhere blind adults navigate—so why has it nearly vanished from mobility training?
medical.summary: For a blind person, braille is more than just a tool for reading books. It's essential for navigating space, and is used on everything from signage to elevator buttons to 3D tactile maps. Yet very few orientation and mobility (O&M) specialists—professionals trained to help people with low vision or blindness move around independently and safely—incorporate braille in their work with adults.

medical.title: Two people, same weight, vastly different futures: New tool exposes obesity danger before disease strikes
medical.summary: The study, published in Nature Medicine, shows that future risk of 18 obesity-related diseases can be predicted using 20 commonly collected health measures, such as blood test results and demographic information. The tool could complement the use of BMI to offer a more accurate, personalized way to identify individuals at a higher risk of developing conditions like heart disease or cancer, leading to better monitoring, earlier interventions, and improved health outcomes.

medical.title: Early outdoor play predicts less sedentary adulthood for people with congenital heart disease
medical.summary: Researchers at University of Tsukuba report that patients with congenital heart disease (CHD) who were more physically active during childhood spend less time engaging in sedentary behavior and participate in higher levels of physical activity in adulthood. These findings, published in the Canadian Journal of Cardiology, may help inform the development of individualized lifestyle interventions for patients with CHD.

medical.title: Scientists discover new way to make drug-resistant cancer treatable again
medical.summary: Cancer cells survive by repairing damage to their DNA—even damage that would normally be fatal. One of their most important defense systems is homologous recombination, a high-precision repair pathway that fixes broken DNA using key proteins such as RAD51 and CHK1. While therapies such as PARP inhibitors have successfully targeted this vulnerability, many tumors eventually regain their DNA repair ability and become resistant to treatment.

medical.title: Task switching raises risk in transplant surgeries, study finds
medical.summary: When transplant surgeons switched between different organ types in consecutive surgeries, one-year mortality rates in patients increased by 14.8%, according to new Virginia Tech research. The findings suggest that scheduling and workflow changes could help reduce these risks—and the implications may extend beyond medicine.

science.title: Autumn leaves transformed into biodegradable mulch film can curb farm plastic pollution
science.summary: Fallen leaves, which are discarded every year, have been transformed into a resource that can replace waste plastics, a major nuisance in rural areas. A research team at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST) has developed biodegradable agricultural vinyl made from fallen leaves, presenting a new way to solve the problem of conventional plastic vinyl, which is a cause of soil pollution. The study is published in the journal Green Chemistry.

science.title: Malaria parasite sneaks mRNA into immune cell nuclei, disrupting defenses
science.summary: RNA technology is regarded as one of the newest frontiers in medicine, but in fact a primordial innovator got there way before we did. The malaria parasite, an ancient single-celled organism, has been using sophisticated RNA maneuvers for millennia. In a study recently published in Cell Reports, researchers at the Weizmann Institute of Science uncover the parasite's RNA strategies—mechanisms that could inspire unexpected applications for RNA-based tools in multiple areas of medicine.

science.title: Chemists unlock two-step alkene alkylation using stable acids and polar coupling
science.summary: Chemists at the Max-Planck-Institut für Kohlenforschung have developed a practical two-step method for alkylating alkenes via thianthrenation, addressing a long-standing synthetic challenge. This breakthrough simplifies complex molecule synthesis with applications in drug discovery, agrochemicals, and materials science. The paper, titled "Decarboxylative Alkylation of Alkenes," is published in Nature.

science.title: Light unlocks full polarization control at ultrafast speeds, reshaping photonics
science.summary: Scientists at Heriot‑Watt University have demonstrated in a world-first, that light can be used to control every aspect of how electromagnetic waves oscillate, opening new technological frontiers. Researchers working in photonics, the science of light, have discovered a new way to control "polarization," a key property of light that plays a crucial role in the performance of technologies such as drug development and quantum computers.

science.title: Sudden quantum jolts may not break adiabatic behavior after all
science.summary: In thermodynamics, an "adiabatic process" is a system change that transfers no heat in or out of the system. Any and all energy change in that system are therefore accomplished by doing work on the system, work being action that moves matter over a distance. (An example is a bicycle tire pump or lifting a box from the floor.)

science.title: Japanese Instagram ads reveal product-specific words that lift click-through rates
science.summary: While research on social media advertising has largely focused on images, videos, and platform algorithms, the role of ad copy has received relatively little attention. This gap is especially evident in non-English contexts, such as Japan. In addition, the same word or expression may be interpreted differently depending on the advertised product.

science.title: Emoji scale shows reliable preschool social skills screening
science.summary: A new test that uses emojis can check the social skills of preschoolers. A vocabulary test is underway.

science.title: Blue carbon: Unearthing the ocean's own climate fix
science.summary: It's a common misconception that rainforests are the lungs of Earth. Our oceans produce most of the oxygen we consume and are more effective at storing carbon.

science.title: Laser-plasma accelerators can preserve polarization of Helium-3 ions
science.summary: Particle accelerators such as those at the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN) in Geneva are typically highly complex large-scale devices. In these ring-shaped facilities, which are often several kilometers in length, magnets and radio-frequency cavities are used to accelerate elementary particles. An alternative approach is now emerging: compact laser–plasma accelerators that can be built and operated at a fraction of the cost. These accelerators can achieve acceleration gradients up to around 1,000 times higher than those of conventional accelerators. Researchers at HHU contributed significantly to this development.

science.title: Odyssey Team Celebrates on a Global Map of Mars
science.summary: Description Team members past and present from NASA’s 2001 Mars Odyssey orbiter mission gathered on April 15, 2026, to celebrate 25 years since the spacecraft’s launch, which took place April 7, 2001. For the occasion, the team rolled out a giant global map of Mars created using imagery from Odyssey’s THEMIS (Thermal Emission Imaging System) […]

science.title: Artemis III Moon Rocket Core Stage on the Move
science.summary: Teams move the core stage, or largest section, of the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket for NASA’s Artemis III mission into the Vehicle Assembly Building at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida in this photo from April 27, 2026. The SLS core stage traveled 900 miles on the Pegasus barge from NASA’s Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans where the stage is manufactured, to complete […]

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: 😂 Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar
mix_items.summary: Lansat în 2006 ca o satiră SF a unei lumi posibile, dar improbabile, filmul Idiocracy a ajuns să fie perceput azi cu totul altfel. Oamenii efectiv au devenit uluitor de proști, așa că pentru cineva din 2026 Idiocracy nu mai are nimic SF în el: totul în film pare banal, e ca și cum te-ai […] The post Oamenii au devenit atât de proști încât Idiocracy a fost mutat de la SF la documentar appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești”
mix_items.summary: United Way România lucrează acolo unde nevoia este cea mai mare, acolo unde sprijinul nu este doar util, ci vital. Fără acest ajutor, mulți copii ar rămâne în urmă, riscând să se piardă în dificultățile vieții. De aceea, apreciem sprijinul pe termen lung pe care Raiffeisen Bank îl oferă programului „Învață să reușești”. Printre copiii […] Articolul Sprijinul Raiffeisen Bank schimbă vieți. Povestea lui Victor din programul „Învață să reușești” apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: In cesarean birth, sex-specific effects occur in microbiota
mix_items.summary: The effects of cesarean delivery on the newborn microbiota appear to vary according to sex. A research team coordinated by INRAE has demonstrated this in a mouse model, showing increased susceptibility to colonic inflammation and a compromised gut barrier in males. These findings are published in Gut Microbes.

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: A study in 1.4 million women expands knowledge on endometriosis and its biological complexity
mix_items.summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease that affects approximately one in ten women of reproductive age—around 190 million worldwide—remains poorly understood from a biological perspective, which has historically hindered both its accurate diagnosis and the development of effective treatments. Now, an international study published in Nature Genetics provides new data to better understand the genetic basis and mechanisms involved in this condition.

mix_items.title: Autumn leaves transformed into biodegradable mulch film can curb farm plastic pollution
mix_items.summary: Fallen leaves, which are discarded every year, have been transformed into a resource that can replace waste plastics, a major nuisance in rural areas. A research team at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST) has developed biodegradable agricultural vinyl made from fallen leaves, presenting a new way to solve the problem of conventional plastic vinyl, which is a cause of soil pollution. The study is published in the journal Green Chemistry.

mix_items.title: Malaria parasite sneaks mRNA into immune cell nuclei, disrupting defenses
mix_items.summary: RNA technology is regarded as one of the newest frontiers in medicine, but in fact a primordial innovator got there way before we did. The malaria parasite, an ancient single-celled organism, has been using sophisticated RNA maneuvers for millennia. In a study recently published in Cell Reports, researchers at the Weizmann Institute of Science uncover the parasite's RNA strategies—mechanisms that could inspire unexpected applications for RNA-based tools in multiple areas of medicine.

mix_items.title: MRI reveals cerebrospinal fluid shifts after mild brain injury
mix_items.summary: Researchers at University of Tsukuba have found that cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) microdynamic motion shows region-specific alterations after mild traumatic brain injury (TBI). Using a specialized magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) technique, the team noninvasively visualized these CSF changes, which have been difficult to quantify with conventional imaging. The approach is expected to advance the understanding of the relationship between post-traumatic brain conditions and cognitive function. The study is published in Frontiers in Neuroscience.

mix_items.title: Treatment of rare childhood epilepsy could begin before birth
mix_items.summary: Research has shown early diagnosis and treatment of epilepsy disorders can improve outcomes. The question of when to administer treatment so it gets ahead of the disease, however, has remained stubbornly elusive. A new Northwestern University study suggests intervention could start during pregnancy—as early as 15 weeks gestation—well before symptoms appear, highlighting the potential benefit of treating certain epilepsy disorders as early as possible.

mix_items.title: Chemists unlock two-step alkene alkylation using stable acids and polar coupling
mix_items.summary: Chemists at the Max-Planck-Institut für Kohlenforschung have developed a practical two-step method for alkylating alkenes via thianthrenation, addressing a long-standing synthetic challenge. This breakthrough simplifies complex molecule synthesis with applications in drug discovery, agrochemicals, and materials science. The paper, titled "Decarboxylative Alkylation of Alkenes," is published in Nature.

mix_items.title: Light unlocks full polarization control at ultrafast speeds, reshaping photonics
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Heriot‑Watt University have demonstrated in a world-first, that light can be used to control every aspect of how electromagnetic waves oscillate, opening new technological frontiers. Researchers working in photonics, the science of light, have discovered a new way to control "polarization," a key property of light that plays a crucial role in the performance of technologies such as drug development and quantum computers.

mix_items.title: No live animal testing needed: Lab-grown airway organoids reveal viral infection in wildlife species
mix_items.summary: Experimental infections, where a pathogen is introduced into the host body to see its effects in action, are considered the gold standard for assessing how vulnerable a host is, offering clear insights into how a pathogen causes disease and spreads through populations. This approach requires deliberately infecting live animals, which not only raises clear ethical concerns but also often proves impractical for many wildlife species, especially endangered ones, where conservation efforts take precedence. Scientists have found a potential solution to this problem.

mix_items.title: Doomscrolling: Tips to stop the scroll, protect your mental health
mix_items.summary: "I should stop." Have you ever said those words to yourself as you scrolled through one disheartening online post after another? This phenomenon is popularly known as doomscrolling. You may wonder: Why is it so difficult to stop doomscrolling once you start? More importantly, what can you do to break free from the negative spiral? Craig Sawchuk, Ph.D., a psychologist at Mayo Clinic in Rochester, offers insights.

mix_items.title: Sudden quantum jolts may not break adiabatic behavior after all
mix_items.summary: In thermodynamics, an "adiabatic process" is a system change that transfers no heat in or out of the system. Any and all energy change in that system are therefore accomplished by doing work on the system, work being action that moves matter over a distance. (An example is a bicycle tire pump or lifting a box from the floor.)

mix_items.title: Japanese Instagram ads reveal product-specific words that lift click-through rates
mix_items.summary: While research on social media advertising has largely focused on images, videos, and platform algorithms, the role of ad copy has received relatively little attention. This gap is especially evident in non-English contexts, such as Japan. In addition, the same word or expression may be interpreted differently depending on the advertised product.

mix_items.title: Emoji scale shows reliable preschool social skills screening
mix_items.summary: A new test that uses emojis can check the social skills of preschoolers. A vocabulary test is underway.

mix_items.title: Braille is everywhere blind adults navigate—so why has it nearly vanished from mobility training?
mix_items.summary: For a blind person, braille is more than just a tool for reading books. It's essential for navigating space, and is used on everything from signage to elevator buttons to 3D tactile maps. Yet very few orientation and mobility (O&M) specialists—professionals trained to help people with low vision or blindness move around independently and safely—incorporate braille in their work with adults.

mix_items.title: Two people, same weight, vastly different futures: New tool exposes obesity danger before disease strikes
mix_items.summary: The study, published in Nature Medicine, shows that future risk of 18 obesity-related diseases can be predicted using 20 commonly collected health measures, such as blood test results and demographic information. The tool could complement the use of BMI to offer a more accurate, personalized way to identify individuals at a higher risk of developing conditions like heart disease or cancer, leading to better monitoring, earlier interventions, and improved health outcomes.

mix_items.title: Early outdoor play predicts less sedentary adulthood for people with congenital heart disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers at University of Tsukuba report that patients with congenital heart disease (CHD) who were more physically active during childhood spend less time engaging in sedentary behavior and participate in higher levels of physical activity in adulthood. These findings, published in the Canadian Journal of Cardiology, may help inform the development of individualized lifestyle interventions for patients with CHD.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover new way to make drug-resistant cancer treatable again
mix_items.summary: Cancer cells survive by repairing damage to their DNA—even damage that would normally be fatal. One of their most important defense systems is homologous recombination, a high-precision repair pathway that fixes broken DNA using key proteins such as RAD51 and CHK1. While therapies such as PARP inhibitors have successfully targeted this vulnerability, many tumors eventually regain their DNA repair ability and become resistant to treatment.

mix_items.title: Blue carbon: Unearthing the ocean's own climate fix
mix_items.summary: It's a common misconception that rainforests are the lungs of Earth. Our oceans produce most of the oxygen we consume and are more effective at storing carbon.

mix_items.title: Task switching raises risk in transplant surgeries, study finds
mix_items.summary: When transplant surgeons switched between different organ types in consecutive surgeries, one-year mortality rates in patients increased by 14.8%, according to new Virginia Tech research. The findings suggest that scheduling and workflow changes could help reduce these risks—and the implications may extend beyond medicine.

mix_items.title: Laser-plasma accelerators can preserve polarization of Helium-3 ions
mix_items.summary: Particle accelerators such as those at the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN) in Geneva are typically highly complex large-scale devices. In these ring-shaped facilities, which are often several kilometers in length, magnets and radio-frequency cavities are used to accelerate elementary particles. An alternative approach is now emerging: compact laser–plasma accelerators that can be built and operated at a fraction of the cost. These accelerators can achieve acceleration gradients up to around 1,000 times higher than those of conventional accelerators. Researchers at HHU contributed significantly to this development.

mix_items.title: Odyssey Team Celebrates on a Global Map of Mars
mix_items.summary: Description Team members past and present from NASA’s 2001 Mars Odyssey orbiter mission gathered on April 15, 2026, to celebrate 25 years since the spacecraft’s launch, which took place April 7, 2001. For the occasion, the team rolled out a giant global map of Mars created using imagery from Odyssey’s THEMIS (Thermal Emission Imaging System) […]

mix_items.title: Artemis III Moon Rocket Core Stage on the Move
mix_items.summary: Teams move the core stage, or largest section, of the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket for NASA’s Artemis III mission into the Vehicle Assembly Building at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida in this photo from April 27, 2026. The SLS core stage traveled 900 miles on the Pegasus barge from NASA’s Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans where the stage is manufactured, to complete […]

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Am facut pasi spre succes. Majoritatea au fost spre bucatarie.

satire_ro.title: Soros riscă să-și piardă nemurirea din cauza natalității scăzute: „Găsesc tot mai greu sânge de copil”
